rule: Write chart titles as a finding that states the main message of the chart, not as a neutral description of the topic.
Never label, caption or number charts, graphs or figures with "Figure 1", "Figure 2", etc.
The title should have a key message not just describe the chart.
Keep chart footers to a maximum of two lines, ideally one line, by shortening references and using superscript symbols where needed.
Keep chart footers to a maximum of two lines, ideally one line, by shortening references and using superscript symbols where needed.
Do not put scenario descriptions or explanations in chart footers; incorporate them into the chart's legend or annotations instead.
Never write the organisation's full name "Transport & Environment" in any publication, including citations; always write "T&E".
Never write the organisation's full name "Transport & Environment" in any publication, including citations; always write "T&E".
Use short, crisp paragraphs so that essential points are not lost in long documents.
Remove waffle: replace overly formal language and delete unnecessary words or phrases.
Remove waffle: replace overly formal language and delete unnecessary words or phrases.
Write persuasively but not shouty: avoid superlatives, emotional language and hyperbole, and rely on objective, clear text.
Write in the active voice rather than the passive voice, unless the active phrasing is awkward.
Refer to the organisation as "we" and "our" in publications, not T&E by name.
After a long sentence that explains or elaborates an idea, follow it with a short sentence to aid readability.
After a long sentence that explains or elaborates an idea, follow it with a short sentence to aid readability.
Keep sentences short: ideally less than 13 words and definitely less than 17 words.
Use the present simple tense by default if in doubt.
Front-load information: state the main result or conclusion in the first sentence, then elaborate and fill in the gaps; assume the reader will not read past the first sentence.
Express one idea per sentence.
Cover one theme per paragraph.

Do not use bold text or underlining for emphasis (especially not combined); use bullet points or reserved space on the page instead. Bold may be used very sparingly and never for whole sentences - if a whole sentence deserves bold, it should be a (sub)section title.
Use unit prefixes with the correct case: uppercase "M" means mega (million); lowercase "m" means metre.
Use "tonne" (symbol "t") for 1,000 kg; never use "ton", which in the US means 2,000 pounds.
Put a space between the number and its measurement unit, but no space before symbols such as degrees or percent.
Write chemical compounds with subscripts and chemical elements with their periodic-table symbols, and put a space between a unit and a compound.
Spell out whole numbers below ten in words, unless the number is paired with a measurement unit; use numerals for 10 to 999,999.
Use a comma as the thousands separator only for numbers of five or more digits (i.e. above 9999), and always use a full stop as the decimal point.
Never start a sentence with a numeral: spell the number out, or better, restructure the sentence (especially for decimals or unrounded numbers).
Never abbreviate millions or billions as "mln", "bln" or similar; write the word out in full.
Use SI prefixes (k, M, T) with technical units.
When listing several amounts, repeat the scale word with every figure; do not let one scale word cover the whole list.
